news_summary: NASA's Artemis II mission achieved a historic milestone, marking significant progress in human space exploration.
news_summary_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA a franchi une étape historique, marquant ainsi une avancée majeure dans l'exploration spatiale humaine.
news_summary_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA ha alcanzado un hito histórico, lo que supone un avance significativo en la exploración espacial tripulada.

articles.title: NASA’s Artemis II mission was a historic success
articles.summary: The astronauts of the Artemis II mission around the moon have made it home safely to Earth, marking the end of a triumphant mission and the beginning of a longer road to stay on the moon
articles.content:
The astronauts of the Artemis II mission around the moon have made it home safely to Earth, marking the end of a triumphant mission and the beginning of a longer road to stay on the moon

NASA

The astronauts of the Artemis II mission have made it home. Their journey, which began in Cape Canaveral, Florida, on 1 April, took them around the moon and further from Earth than any human has travelled before. On 10 April, they splashed down in their capsule off the coast of California. On landing, mission commander Reid Wiseman reported “four green crew members”, indicating that he, his NASA crewmates Victor Glover and Christina Koch and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen were all feeling good after re-entering Earth’s atmosphere. “What a journey,” he said.

This historic flight marked the first time humans have been to the moon since the Apollo 18 mission in 1972. At its most distant, the Orion capsule was 406,771 kilometres from Earth, beating the distance record set by the crew of the Apollo 13 mission in 1970.

Advertisement

The main purpose of Artemis II was to act as a test flight for future lunar missions, a test it passed with flying colours, but there were several science goals as well. Several of them were tied to the hues of the lunar surface – it appears simply grey from afar, but up close the astronauts noted green, brown and even orange hues. These observations can help scientists unravel the composition and history of the lunar surface, particularly on its far side, where the astronauts observed some areas that have never been directly seen by human eyes before.

When Earth and the moon were both visible at once, though, the brightness of sunlight reflected off the planet in a phenomenon called earthshine drowned out these subtle variations. “The moon turned into a sponge of light,” said Koch. “As soon as the Earth got close enough to be in my field of view to take them both in at the same time, [the moon] dulled, it turned into a sponge, it’s almost like it went matte.” In fact, the earthshine through one of the windows throughout the flight was so bright that the crew covered the window with a spare shirt, prompting calls for future missions to include dedicated window shades.

The astronauts also took pictures and made voice recordings as they watched the moon go by, including detailed descriptions of the terrain below them. “It’s those kind of nuanced observations that could ultimately inform future landed missions, future crewed missions, to understand where [we can] go to maximise the scientific value,” said NASA’s Artemis science lead, Kelsey Young, in a 7 April press conference. “These ultimately get at chronology of the solar system, at how the inner solar system has evolved over time, which connects to the moon being the witness plate for our planet and for the inner solar system.”

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

While their spacecraft was behind the moon, the Artemis crew also got to witness a solar eclipse unlike any visible from Earth, in which the sun appeared smaller than the moon in the sky as it disappeared behind the lunar horizon. “The eclipse occurred, and then we had 5 minutes of human emotional reaction to staring at that orb floating in the vastness of space,” said Wiseman. “Then right after that, somebody in the cabin said, ‘Let’s look for impact flashes,’ and immediately we saw one or two or three.”

Spotting impact flashes, which are momentary sparks of light caused by meteorites hitting the darkened surface of the moon, was one of the mission’s science goals, because observing them can help us figure out how concerned future missions to the surface should be about meteorite impacts.

The next mission in the Artemis programme, Artemis III, won’t include a landing or even a visit to the moon, after a series of recent changes aimed at making the programme nimbler and more practical. Instead, it will primarily be about testing the Orion capsule’s ability to dock with lunar landers in orbit around Earth. It is planned for 2027; the next lunar landing is scheduled for the Artemis IV mission in 2028.

All of these flights are part of a larger goal to build a sustained human presence on the moon. NASA recently announced a pivot in its plans, from a space station in lunar orbit to a base on the ground, and China’s space programme has similar aspirations. The hope is that within a few decades, going to the moon will be as relatively routine as visiting the International Space Station is now – and eventually, maybe as routine as a transcontinental flight. Whether or not that happens, the images from Artemis II have been emblazoned on the public consciousness, injecting new life into our view of the moon.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the successful completion of NASA's Artemis II mission, a historic human spaceflight around the moon marking the furthest humans have traveled from Earth since 1972. It includes significant scientific observations about the lunar surface, meteorite impacts, and sets the stage for future sustained human presence on the moon. The mission has broad significance for space exploration and humanity's future in space, with detailed context and clear positive impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis II mission returns safely after historic moon orbit
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Artemis II mission was launched to send astronauts around the moon, testing new spacecraft and gathering important scientific data. It was the first human trip past the moon since 1972.

**What happened** – On April 1, four astronauts took off from Florida and traveled farther from Earth than anyone has in over 50 years. They orbited the moon, made detailed observations, and returned safely to Earth on April 10, splashing down off California. The crew included NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen.

**Impact** – This mission broke distance records and gave scientists new information about the moon’s surface colors and meteorite impacts. For example, the astronauts noticed green, brown, and orange shades on the moon, which could help us understand its history. They also witnessed a rare solar eclipse from space and spotted flashes made by meteorites hitting the moon. These findings will guide future missions and help plan where humans might live on the moon.

**What's next step** – The next Artemis missions will continue testing spacecraft and aim for a moon landing by 2028. NASA plans to build a base on the moon’s surface for long-term human visits, making moon travel more common in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II successfully tested new space technology and gathered fresh insights about the moon, paving the way for humans to live there one day.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA revient en toute sécurité après une orbite historique autour de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA avait pour but d’envoyer des astronautes autour de la Lune pour tester un nouveau vaisseau spatial et recueillir des données scientifiques importantes. C’était le premier voyage humain au-delà de la Lune depuis 1972.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er avril, quatre astronautes ont décollé de Floride et ont voyagé plus loin de la Terre que depuis plus de 50 ans. Ils ont fait le tour de la Lune, observé en détail sa surface, puis sont revenus en toute sécurité sur Terre le 10 avril, en amerrissant au large de la Californie. L’équipage comprenait Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch de la NASA, et l’astronaute canadien Jeremy Hansen.

**Impact** – Cette mission a battu des records de distance et a offert aux scientifiques de nouvelles informations sur les couleurs de la surface lunaire et les impacts de météorites. Par exemple, les astronautes ont remarqué des teintes vertes, brunes et orange sur la Lune, ce qui peut aider à comprendre son histoire. Ils ont aussi observé une éclipse solaire rare depuis l’espace et vu des éclairs causés par des météorites frappant la Lune. Ces découvertes guideront les futures missions et aideront à planifier où les humains pourraient vivre sur la Lune.

**Prochaine étape** – Les prochaines missions Artemis continueront à tester les vaisseaux spatiaux et viseront un atterrissage sur la Lune en 2028. La NASA prévoit de construire une base sur la surface lunaire pour des visites humaines à long terme, rendant les voyages lunaires plus fréquents.

**Phrase clé** – Artemis II a testé avec succès de nouvelles technologies spatiales et apporté de nouvelles connaissances sur la Lune, ouvrant la voie à la vie humaine sur place un jour.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA regresa con seguridad tras una histórica órbita lunar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA tenía como objetivo enviar astronautas alrededor de la Luna para probar una nueva nave espacial y recopilar datos científicos importantes. Fue el primer viaje humano más allá de la Luna desde 1972.

**Qué pasó** – El 1 de abril, cuatro astronautas despegaron desde Florida y viajaron más lejos de la Tierra que en más de 50 años. Orbitaron la Luna, hicieron observaciones detalladas y regresaron sanos y salvos a la Tierra el 10 de abril, amerizando frente a la costa de California. La tripulación incluía a los astronautas de la NASA Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, y al astronauta canadiense Jeremy Hansen.

**Impacto** – Esta misión rompió récords de distancia y proporcionó a los científicos nueva información sobre los colores de la superficie lunar y los impactos de meteoritos. Por ejemplo, los astronautas notaron tonos verdes, marrones y naranjas en la Luna, lo que puede ayudar a entender su historia. También presenciaron un eclipse solar raro desde el espacio y vieron destellos causados por meteoritos al impactar la Luna. Estos hallazgos guiarán futuras misiones y ayudarán a planificar dónde podrían vivir los humanos en la Luna.

**Próximo paso** – Las próximas misiones Artemis continuarán probando naves espaciales y apuntan a un aterrizaje lunar en 2028. La NASA planea construir una base en la superficie lunar para visitas humanas a largo plazo, haciendo que viajar a la Luna sea más común.

**Frase clave** – Artemis II probó con éxito nueva tecnología espacial y aportó nuevos conocimientos sobre la Luna, preparando el camino para que los humanos vivan allí algún día.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sleek spacecraft silhouette orbiting a softly glowing, textured moon showing subtle green, brown, and orange hues on its far side, with a distant Earth shining brightly in the background casting a gentle earthshine glow, all set against the deep blackness of space with tiny sparkling stars.
